envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Industrial Process Furnace and Oven Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Engineered-to-order and standard capital equipment sold through quotations and projects, followed by recurring parts, field service, calibration, maintenance, modernization, software and controls work across a long-lived installed base.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off equipment builders without meaningful repeat or aftermarket customer relationships, captive internal engineering shops, heat-treating service bureaus classified elsewhere, cement, chemical and wood-kiln equipment excluded by NAICS, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market industrial furnace, oven, kiln, induction-heating, and dielectric-heating OEMs with repeat external revenue from installed-base parts, preventive maintenance, calibration, field service, technical support, controls upgrades, retrofits, rebuilds, and repeat equipment orders.

a.bridge: BLS measures all machinery manufacturing rather than NAICS 333994 and reports occupations, not task-level AI substitutability. I map much of engineering analysis and documentation, estimating, project coordination, software and controls work, purchasing, technical support, sales support, planning, and office administration to partial exposure, while treating fabrication, assembly, wiring, installation, field repair, testing, and material handling as minimally exposed. Furnace OEMs may have more engineering and project labor than broad machinery, biasing the broad-sector estimate downward, but safety-critical custom work may be less substitutable than generic digital tasks, biasing it upward.
caveats: The occupation mix is for broad NAICS 333 rather than furnace and oven manufacturers.
No source separates already automated CAD, ERP, and controls workflows from the remaining opportunity.
The injected compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis as stated in the assignment.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit, wage-weighted occupation extract plus time studies from engineering, estimating, controls, project management, production, and aftermarket teams at 15 furnace OEMs would materially change the range.
a.rationale: In broad machinery manufacturing, architecture and engineering is 10.35% of employment, management 7.70%, business and financial operations 5.83%, computer and mathematical 2.37%, sales 3.50%, and office support 8.50%, alongside production at 49.46%. The industry therefore has a substantial high-wage digital layer suitable for AI-assisted design reuse, specifications, proposals, manuals, controls code, project administration, troubleshooting, and parts identification, but roughly half the workforce remains in physical production.

d5.bridge: DOE measures process heat's role in manufacturing and electrification potential, Census reports broad durable-goods orders, and OEM service pages show long equipment lives and modernization needs; none directly forecasts constant-price demand for the lens's furnace equipment and lifecycle services. I translate essential process-heat use, aging installed bases, energy-efficiency upgrades and electrification into modest growth, tempered by cyclical capital spending. Broad durable-goods orders can overstate volatility relative to aftermarket service, while policy-driven electrification evidence may overstate adoption if power costs and capital budgets delay projects.
caveats: Durable-goods orders are much broader and volatile month to month.
DOE energy shares establish importance, not equipment-purchase timing.
Electrification may replace combustion furnaces, creating product-mix shifts and stranded engineering capabilities.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted US shipments and order backlogs for 333994, installed-base age, furnace retrofit and replacement rates, service revenue, and customer capital budgets by end market through 2031 would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Process heating is essential across metals, glass, ceramics, chemicals and other production and accounts for 63% of manufacturing energy use by DOE's measure. Efficiency, sensing, controls, emissions and electrification needs create retrofit and replacement demand, while long furnace lives support recurring maintenance. The May 2026 fall in total durable-goods orders and manufacturing cyclicality justify downside below current demand.

caveats: Public OEM websites demonstrate offered services but not revenue mix or customer recurrence.
The injected 107-firm count is a margin-bridged ESTIMATE rather than directly observed EBITDA-band firms.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of target-band 333994 companies showing aftermarket share, repeat-customer revenue, installed-base size, external sales, ownership independence, and normalized EBITDA would materially improve eligibility.
e.rationale: Multiple furnace OEMs publicly offer lifecycle parts, maintenance, repair, technical support, calibration, rebuilds and modernization, so repeat installed-base revenue is common. Eligibility is reduced for firms dominated by episodic new-equipment projects, captive units, distributors without manufacturing, and OEMs whose customer, engineering, or service relationships cannot transfer independently.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 333994 ($847,497,397) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 333994 ($2,885,784,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $293,211 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 333994); class <5: 63 firms, avg revenue/firm $906,937 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $177,941 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 42 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,672,262 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $328,098 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 58 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,651,103 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $716,346 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 92 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,240,098 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,401,507 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $44,990,933 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,827,221 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $57,361,154 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $11,254,258 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 107

caveats: Operator-required does not mean current labor intensity persists; automated OEMs still count as operators.
Open controls and additive manufacturing of spares could shift work away from incumbent OEMs.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement studies showing in-house design and maintenance, standardized modular furnace adoption, third-party controls platforms, remote self-service resolution, and OEM-disintermediation rates would materially move the estimate.
o.rationale: Surviving demand requires an accountable operator to engineer, fabricate, integrate, test, install, calibrate, repair and warrant safety-critical physical equipment. Software can make design, control, monitoring and troubleshooting more self-service, and large customers can internalize some engineering, but it cannot manufacture or assume responsibility for the furnace and its thermal performance.

q.bridge: OEM service pages document quoted equipment, parts, preventive maintenance, calibration, repair, technical support and engineered retrofits, not the discounted share of AI benefit retained. I infer that fixed project and parts pricing initially decouples customer price from internal labor hours, while installed-base knowledge and downtime urgency support retention. I then haircut for competitive bidding, customer procurement, project change orders, negotiated service agreements, and price competition. This may overstate retention on competitively bid new systems and understate it for proprietary emergency parts and controls support.
caveats: Public service menus do not disclose pricing, discounts, utilization or gross margins.
Revenue models differ between standard ovens, large custom lines, emergency repair and maintenance agreements.
Implementation difficulty and demand effects are excluded from this primitive.
q.change_evidence: Contract, quote, realized-price, gross-margin and renewal cohorts before and after automation, split among new equipment, parts, field service, calibration, controls upgrades and rebuilds, would materially move retention.
q.rationale: Customers buy equipment performance, uptime, compliance and technical outcomes rather than transparent labor inputs. Better engineering reuse, quoting and support can therefore expand margin without automatic pass-through. Retention is strongest in proprietary parts and urgent aftermarket work and weaker in large new-equipment bids and standardized services.

caveats: This is bounded implementation judgment rather than an observed adoption rate.
Small OEMs may have simpler workflows but weaker data infrastructure than global manufacturers.
rho.change_evidence: Audited five-year productivity and error data from furnace OEM deployments covering engineering hours, quote cycle time, controls commissioning, support resolution, rework, and field-service utilization would materially move implementation.
rho.rationale: Knowledge retrieval, proposal drafting, prior-design reuse, document generation, project reporting, parts matching, support triage, scheduling, and bounded controls assistance can be deployed with human approval. Full realization is constrained by fragmented legacy drawings, proprietary customer requirements, export controls, cybersecurity, combustion and electrical safety, professional judgment, site-specific integration, validation, and liability for thermal-process failures.

s5.bridge: BizBuySell reports completed sales for small manufacturers across industries and generally below the target EBITDA band, while Census owner-age evidence is economy-wide. I translate the observed manufacturing transfer market and succession context into a five-year probability for eligible furnace OEMs, then discount for concentrated technical knowledge, long project liabilities, bonding and warranty obligations, export controls, customer qualification, and reliance on key engineers. Broker selection can overstate liquidity; confidential strategic transactions can understate it.
caveats: Marketplace deals skew smaller and less engineered than the target population.
Owner age does not directly measure sale intent or business transferability.
Internal reorganizations and asset-only sales are not qualifying transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed 333994 transactions from GF Data, DealStats, PitchBook, SBA lending, and strategic buyer disclosures matched to the eligible-firm denominator would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Installed bases, engineering libraries, brands, and aftermarket relationships are transferable assets, and manufacturing businesses demonstrably sell. However, a qualifying control transfer must preserve engineering, controls, field-service and customer teams rather than merely sell fabrication assets, keeping availability moderate.

principal_driver: High-value engineering and installed-base aftermarket workflows create repeat, AI-addressable labor around indispensable physical equipment.
principal_weakness: Demand and cash flow can be dominated by lumpy custom projects, while safety-critical validation limits autonomous implementation.
